title: Veal paprikash
subtitle: Hungarian veal in a sweet paprika and soured-cream sauce
excerpt: Tender veal shoulder braised in a generous amount of sweet paprika and finished with crème fraîche. Served with nokedli or egg noodles, it is the lighter cousin of chicken paprikash, delicate, deeply savoury, and deeply orange.
sourceNotes: Based on the Hungarian paprikás tradition as documented in George Lang, 'The Cuisine of Hungary' (1971), which draws on historical Hungarian cookery manuscripts and the work of Karoly Gundel. Sweet paprika is the backbone of the sauce; the sourness comes from crème fraîche stirred in off the heat at the end.

content.text: Paprikás borjú sits in the same family as chicken paprikash, but comes out lighter. Veal is mild and milky, so the paprika comes through clean, without the richer fat and bone notes of chicken thighs. You get a sauce that is almost coral in colour, bright and aromatic. The veal braises down to a tender give over an hour on the hob.

The critical rule with this dish is to never let the paprika burn. It goes into the pot after the onion has softened and before any liquid, and it needs about 30 seconds on the fat to bloom its fragrance, not more. Once it starts to darken it turns bitter. Have the stock ready to pour in immediately after.

The crème fraîche goes in right at the end, off the heat, stirred slowly to make a smooth, creamy sauce. Boiling it in will cause the cream to split. The dish is traditional with nokedli (tiny Hungarian egg-flour dumplings made by pressing a loose batter through a colander) but broad egg noodles or simple boiled potatoes work just as well.

items.name: Beef chuck
items.prepNote: veal shoulder if available; beef chuck is a reliable substitute, cut into 4 cm pieces

content.text: A wide casserole or Dutch oven spreads the heat evenly and gives the onions room to soften without steaming. For the veal: shoulder is the right cut, lean enough not to be fatty, tough enough to benefit from an hour of braising.

Melt {{unsalted-butter}} of butter in the Dutch oven over a medium heat. Add {{onion}} finely chopped onions and cook, stirring occasionally, for 12 to 15 minutes until fully softened and starting to turn pale gold. Add {{garlic}} of crushed garlic and cook for another 2 minutes.

Add {{paprika-sweet}} of sweet paprika and {{tomato-puree}} of tomato purée to the pot and stir for 30 seconds, the paprika should smell fragrant but not toasty. Pour in {{stock-chicken}} of chicken stock immediately to stop the paprika cooking further.

Season {{beef-chuck}} pieces of veal shoulder (or beef chuck) with {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt and {{black-pepper-ground}} of pepper. Add them to the pot in a single layer as best you can. Bring to a gentle simmer, cover with a lid slightly ajar, and cook over a low heat for 45 to 55 minutes until the meat is tender when pressed with a fork. Top up with a splash of water if the liquid reduces too far.

Take the pot off the heat. Stir {{creme-fraiche}} of crème fraîche into the sauce, working it in slowly until the sauce is smooth and uniformly creamy. Taste for seasoning. Serve immediately over egg noodles, nokedli, or plain boiled potatoes.

items.fix: The dish cannot be saved once the paprika has burnt. Start again, keeping the heat at medium and adding the stock within 30 to 40 seconds of the paprika going in.
Always take the pot off the heat before adding the crème fraîche. Stir in slowly. If it splits slightly, a vigorous stir will often bring it back.
items.cause: Crème fraîche added while the pot was still on the heat
items.symptom: Cream has curdled in the sauce

items.fix: Continue braising at a low simmer, checking every 10 minutes. Shoulder improves up to 90 minutes of cooking.
items.cause: Not enough braising time, or heat too high

items.fix: Remove the lid and simmer uncovered for 10 minutes before adding the crème fraîche.
items.cause: Lid fully closed trapping too much steam

content.text: Paprikás is one of the core dishes of the Hungarian kitchen. It is built around the country's most important spice export. The sweet paprika grown around Kalocsa and Szeged in southern Hungary has a gentle, rounded fruitiness, different from the smoked Spanish kind. Veal paprikash sits on the menus of the grand old Budapest restaurants (Gundel especially), but it is also an everyday family dish. It is cooked through the week across central Hungary and in the Hungarian communities of Slovakia, Romania, and Austria.
